findings.title: JSON.stringify used for change detection — unreliable for object equality
findings.description: saveEditing() uses `JSON.stringify(nextData) === JSON.stringify(log.data ?? {})` as a short-circuit to skip saves when nothing changed. JSON.stringify is order-sensitive for object keys and does not handle undefined fields consistently. If the server returns data with different key ordering than the client-constructed nextData, the comparison will falsely fire a save (or vice versa). For digestion logs the risk is particularly high because nextData is fully reconstructed from scratch, not derived from log.data, so the key order may differ even when values are identical.
findings.fix: Remove the JSON.stringify comparison entirely, or replace it with a field-by-field check specific to each log type. The safest default is to always call onSave when the user explicitly clicks Save — the mutation is idempotent and the UX cost of a redundant write is negligible.

findings.title: Save error silently swallowed — no user feedback on failure
findings.description: The catch block in saveEditing() is empty: `catch { // Keep editor open so the user can adjust and retry. }`. The editor stays open, which is correct, but the user receives no indication that the save failed. On a clinical app tracking health data, a silent failure is dangerous — the user may believe data was saved when it was not.
findings.fix: Add error state: `const [saveError, setSaveError] = useState<string | null>(null)`. In the catch, set a message. Render it beneath the Save button. Clear it on successful save or when editing is cancelled.

findings.title: EventHabitRow fires multiple concurrent deletes with no error handling
findings.description: The 'Uncheck' button in EventHabitRow iterates over all group entries and calls `void onDelete(entry.id)` for each, in a for-of loop with no await and no error handling. If any individual delete fails, the failure is silently swallowed (the `void` operator discards the promise). If the group has 3 entries, 3 concurrent mutations fire. A partial failure leaves phantom entries in the log without any user notification.
findings.fix: Use `Promise.allSettled` and surface errors to the user. If any delete fails, show an error state on the row rather than leaving stale entries with no feedback. Example: `const results = await Promise.allSettled(group.entries.map(e => onDelete(e.id))); const failed = results.filter(r => r.status === 'rejected'); if (failed.length > 0) { /* show error */ }`

findings.title: Non-cycle reproductive log types are silently dropped
findings.description: In groupLogEntries(), reproductive logs are only pushed to reproductiveEntries when `entryType === 'cycle'`. Any other entryType (e.g. a future 'symptom' or 'note' entry type) falls through to the `unknown type fallback` at line 102, which wraps it as an `individual` item. This is likely wrong: reproductive entries that are not 'cycle' type will render with the generic LogEntry instead of the reproductive-specific UI, with no warning.
findings.fix: Either push all reproductive logs into reproductiveEntries regardless of entryType, or add an explicit fallback that at least logs a warning in development. If entryType filtering is intentional, add a comment explaining what happens to other entryType values and add a dev-mode console.warn.

findings.title: LogEntry receives onDelete/onSave props even though TodayLogActionsContext also provides them
findings.description: TodayLogActionsContext exists specifically to avoid prop-drilling onDelete/onSave through every group row. However, the `individual` case in TodayLog's switch still passes onDelete and onSave as explicit props to <LogEntry>. LogEntry's LogEntryProps type declares these as required props. Meanwhile, child sub-rows inside group rows (FoodSubRow, HabitSubRow, etc.) presumably read from context. This is inconsistent: individual entries use props, grouped sub-rows use context. If the context value and the prop value ever diverge, individual LogEntry items will silently use the stale prop.
findings.fix: Either (a) have LogEntry always consume from context and remove onDelete/onSave from LogEntryProps, or (b) remove TodayLogActionsContext and pass props everywhere. Currently the codebase does both — that is the contradiction. Option (a) is the cleaner direction since the context already exists.

findings.title: TodayLogDataContext is defined but never provided in TodayLog
findings.description: TodayLogContext.tsx defines TodayLogDataProvider and useTodayLogData for sharing habits and weightUnit via context. However, TodayLog.tsx never mounts a <TodayLogDataProvider>. Any component that calls useTodayLogData() will throw 'useTodayLogData must be used within TodayLogDataProvider'. This context is dead infrastructure — either it is not yet wired up (unfinished work) or it was abandoned in favour of prop-drilling, but the context definition was not cleaned up.
findings.fix: Either (a) mount <TodayLogDataProvider value={{ habits, weightUnit }}> in TodayLog and migrate group rows to use useTodayLogData() rather than receiving habits as a prop, or (b) delete TodayLogDataContext and useTodayLogData from the file.

findings.title: formatItemDisplay and getFoodItemDisplayName are near-duplicate implementations
findings.description: helpers.ts contains two functions that do essentially the same thing: resolve a food item's display name including quantity and unit. getFoodItemDisplayName (line 64) and formatItemDisplay (line 137) have identical logic with minor differences in formatting (the latter omits a space between qty and unit in one branch). Both are exported and consumers appear to use them interchangeably. This creates drift risk: a bug fix in one will not be applied to the other.
findings.fix: Consolidate into one function. getFoodItemDisplayName has a cleaner signature (typed FoodItem parameter). formatItemDisplay can be an alias or removed. Update all call sites.

findings.title: Food item list uses unstable key derived from draft name
findings.description: The food item editor list uses `key={\`draft-${draft.name || i}\`}`. When draft.name is empty (new blank item, or the user just cleared a field), the key falls back to the index `i`. Mixing name-based and index-based keys within the same render cycle causes React to remount inputs unexpectedly when items are reordered or deleted, losing focus and draft state mid-edit.
findings.fix: Assign a stable, immutable ID when draft items are initialised — e.g., use the original item index or a UUID: `const [draftItems, setDraftItems] = useState(() => initFoodDraftItems().map((item, i) => ({ ...item, _key: i })))`. Use `_key` as the React key. Never derive keys from mutable draft content.

findings.title: Bristol code derivation in saveEditing is inconsistent with the 1–7 spec
findings.description: When saving a digestion log, consistencyTag is derived: `if (draftBristol >= 7) 'diarrhea'; else if (draftBristol >= 6) 'loose'; else if (draftBristol > 0) 'firm'`. Bristol codes 1–5 are all tagged 'firm', which loses clinical nuance. Codes 1–2 are typically 'hard/constipated', 3–4 are normal, 5 is 'soft but formed'. Lumping all into 'firm' is factually wrong and will corrupt downstream analysis.
findings.fix: Align with the Bristol Stool Scale: 1–2 → 'hard', 3–4 → 'normal', 5 → 'soft', 6 → 'loose', 7 → 'diarrhea'. Check the domain types for accepted consistencyTag values and ensure the mapping matches.

findings.title: getHabitIcon called twice per render for habit logs
findings.description: For habit-type logs, the component calls `getHabitIcon(habitConfig)` twice in the same render: once to destructure Icon and once to get toneClassName (lines 77–78). If getHabitIcon is non-trivial, this is wasted computation every render.
findings.fix: Call it once and destructure both values: `const { Icon, toneClassName: color } = habitConfig ? getHabitIcon(habitConfig) : { Icon: getLogIcon(log), toneClassName: getLogColor(log) };`

findings.title: Fluid name editing limited to 10 characters — likely too short
findings.description: The fluid name input has `maxLength={10}`. Common fluid names like 'sports drink', 'coconut water', or 'herbal tea' exceed this. The food item name input has `maxLength={60}`. The 10-character limit appears to be an accidental placeholder, not a deliberate UX decision.
findings.fix: Raise maxLength to at least 40 to match common fluid names. If there is a deliberate constraint (e.g., display truncation), document it in a comment.

findings.title: Motion chevron in digestion row is missing aria-hidden
findings.description: The ChevronDown inside the motion.div at line 363 is a purely decorative rotation indicator. It has no aria-label or aria-hidden attribute. The parent button already has aria-expanded, so the chevron is redundant for screen readers but will be announced as an unlabelled interactive element in some AT configurations.
findings.fix: The motion.div already has `aria-hidden='true'` — verify this is being serialised to the DOM correctly by motion. If motion strips aria attributes, add it to the ChevronDown instead: `<ChevronDown aria-hidden='true' />`

findings.title: Habit group key falls back to the string 'habit' for all unidentifiable habits
findings.description: When a habit log has neither habitId nor name, all such entries get grouped together under the key 'habit'. If a user has multiple distinct habits that both lack a habitId and a name, they will be incorrectly merged into one group. This is unlikely in practice but could surface after data migrations or if the habit-log writing path has a bug.
findings.fix: Use `log.id` as a final fallback key (creating individual groups) rather than the static string 'habit'. Or at minimum, add a dev-mode console.warn when this branch is reached.

findings.title: activity and sleep cases in switch are identical — can be collapsed
findings.description: The 'activity' and 'sleep' cases (lines 260–277) render the same <ActivityGroupRow> component with identical props. The duplicated block is noise that will diverge if ActivityGroupRow's interface changes.

findings.title: cancelEditing duplicates startEditing state resets
findings.description: cancelEditing() manually resets draftPrimary, draftNotes, draftDate, and draftTimestamp — but startEditing() already sets all of these. The cancel path does not reset draftItems, draftBristol, draftEpisodes, etc. If a user edits a digestion entry and cancels, the abandoned draft bristol/urgency/effort values remain in state. On the next expand, handleToggleExpand will re-initialise them from log.data, so the visible behaviour is correct, but the stale state is a latent risk.
findings.fix: Extract draft initialisation into a single resetDraft() function. Call it from both startEditing and cancelEditing. This ensures cancellation is always complete.

findings.title: LogEntry carries 14 pieces of useState — too many responsibilities in one component
findings.description: LogEntry handles display, inline editing for food, fluid, digestion, reproductive, and habit/activity log types — each with their own draft fields — plus delete confirmation, saving state, and auto-edit triggering. It has at least 14 useState calls. This component is doing too much. Editing logic for each log type should be extracted into type-specific edit components (FoodEditor, DigestivEditor, etc.) that are conditionally rendered.
findings.fix: Refactor: extract DigestiveEditor, FoodEditor, FluidEditor, ReproductiveEditor as separate components. LogEntry becomes a thin dispatcher. This is a significant refactor, not a one-liner, so treat as a tracked debt item.
